askNext: What information is deliberately preserved during compaction?
Can the system recover the original transcript or evidence?
How are uncertainties and unresolved blockers carried forward?
context-compaction.body: Compaction is where continuity becomes editorial. Treat the editor as fallible.
context-compaction.example: "The context compaction kept the deployment command and forgot why it was forbidden, which is how efficiency becomes a postmortem heading."
context-compaction.misuse: It gets treated as lossless memory. It is not. Compaction preserves a judgement about what mattered, and that judgement can omit caveats, uncertainty, failed attempts, and inconvenient constraints.
context-compaction.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what to keep, what to discard, how to mark uncertainty, and how to recover original evidence when the compacted version starts behaving like an unreliable witness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lossy summary of working state, useful only if everyone remembers it is lossy.

context-compaction.whyExists: Long-running agents and assistants accumulate instructions, tool results, decisions, and user preferences. Compaction keeps the session usable when the raw transcript would exceed limits or become too costly to carry forward.

askNext: What content is eligible for clearing, and what must remain immutable?
How does editing affect prompt caching, cost, and reproducibility?
Can operators inspect the removed content after the model can no longer see it?
context-editing.body: Every long-running conversation eventually needs an editor. The difficult part is ensuring the editor has better judgement than the accumulation.
context-editing.example: "Context editing removed the first twelve search results and retained the decision they had supported, which was either efficient or ominous depending on the audit trail."
context-editing.misuse: It is sold as memory management while sometimes functioning as deletion with reassuring typography. Clearing the wrong evidence can break continuity, hide an earlier constraint, invalidate prompt caches, or leave the model confidently reasoning from the placeholder.
context-editing.practicalMeaning: Define thresholds and retention rules, clear older low-value blocks, and keep enough recent state for the task. Measure task quality, token savings, cache invalidation, and whether audit logs retain what the model no longer sees.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Remove stale context deliberately, preserve the audit trail elsewhere, and verify that the agent still remembers the instruction that mattered.

context-editing.warningLabel: A shorter context can also be a shorter memory.
context-editing.whyExists: Long-running agents accumulate file contents, search results, tool traces, and reasoning state until cost rises or the context limit approaches. Curating stale material can preserve useful working space without restarting the entire session.

askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?
How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped?
Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly?
context-engineering.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.
context-engineering.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
context-engineering.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
context-engineering.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

context-engineering.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.
context-engineering.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.

askNext: Which sources are allowed to enter context for this user, task, and role?
What keeps sensitive, stale, poisoned, or irrelevant material outside the prompt?
Can the system explain what crossed the perimeter for a disputed answer?
context-perimeter.body: The context perimeter is where the cheerful phrase "give it more context" starts receiving questions from security.
context-perimeter.example: "The support agent needed a tighter context perimeter, not another million tokens of customer history and hope."
context-perimeter.misuse: It is weakened when teams treat context as a bigger bucket rather than a governed boundary. More context can mean more leakage, more poisoning, more stale assumptions, and more authority accidentally handed to the model.
context-perimeter.practicalMeaning: In practice it means defining allowed sources, excluding sensitive or irrelevant material, filtering retrieved chunks, separating tenants and roles, pruning stale memory, and logging what crossed the boundary when an answer mattered.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The boundary that decides what information may reach the model for this task.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The place where prompt engineering becomes access control, memory hygiene, and audit logging.

context-perimeter.warningLabel: A larger context window is not a larger permission model.
context-perimeter.whyExists: AI systems assemble answers from many sources: user prompts, documents, memory, tools, policies, and previous turns. The perimeter names the control surface for deciding what belongs inside that assembled working context.

askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?
context-poisoning.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.
context-poisoning.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
context-poisoning.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
context-poisoning.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.
context-poisoning.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

context-poisoning.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.
context-poisoning.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.

askNext: What exact meaning of Context pruning is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
context-pruning.body: Context pruning gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around State Compaction keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
context-pruning.example: "Once context pruning entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
context-pruning.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
context-pruning.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

context-pruning.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter?
Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph?
Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it?
context-rot.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.
context-rot.example: "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
context-rot.misuse: The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
context-rot.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The working context has become too bloated, stale, or contradictory to be useful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start a new thread before the old one becomes a constitutional monarchy of bad assumptions.

context-rot.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.

askNext: What exact meaning of Context Security Incident Response Statement is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
context-security-incident-response-state.body: Context Security Incident Response Statement earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
context-security-incident-response-state.example: "Context Security Incident Response Statement came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
context-security-incident-response-state.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
context-security-incident-response-state.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

context-security-incident-response-state.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
context-security-incident-response-state.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Context.ai often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all?
What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion?
Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text?
context-stuffing.body: Context stuffing is one of the first failure modes granted by abundance. Once the window grows large enough, restraint starts looking less like engineering and more like an avoidable meeting.
context-stuffing.example: "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
context-stuffing.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
context-stuffing.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

context-stuffing.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.

askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?
context-window.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.
context-window.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
context-window.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
context-window.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

context-window.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.

askNext: What exact meaning of Context Workspace Engine is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
context-workspace-engine.body: Context Workspace Engine earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
context-workspace-engine.example: "Context Workspace Engine came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."

context-workspace-engine.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
context-workspace-engine.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Context.ai often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite?
Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary?
Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions?
context-ai.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.
context-ai.example: "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
context-ai.misuse: By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
context-ai.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.
context-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

context-ai.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.
context-ai.whyExists: Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.

askNext: What is the domain corpus licence and quality?
How do you measure forgetting?
Is instruction tuning planned after?
continued-pretraining.body: Continued pretraining is a second childhood for domain text. Childhood needs supervision.
continued-pretraining.example: "Continued pretraining taught clinical abbreviations and temporarily untaught polite refusals."
continued-pretraining.misuse: Confused with fine-tuning on Q&A pairs. Different objective, different data needs, different failure modes like catastrophic forgetting.
continued-pretraining.practicalMeaning: Curate domain corpora carefully, mix with general data if needed, and evaluate both domain gains and general regressions before instruction tuning.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Continued pretraining (also called domain-adaptive pretraining) further trains a base model with a language-modelling objective on additional corpora before instruction tuning or alignment.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Curate domain corpora carefully, mix with general data if needed, and evaluate both domain gains and general regressions before instruction tuning.

continued-pretraining.warningLabel: More pretraining is not free specialisation.
continued-pretraining.whyExists: General models lack specialised distributional knowledge. Continued pretraining injects domain text without jumping straight to supervised tasks.

askNext: What limits concurrency: KV memory, compute, or rate limits?
Are interactive and batch jobs isolated or fighting for the same batch slots?
Which latency percentile regressed when utilisation went up?
continuous-batching.body: Continuous batching is queue theory with a CUDA accent.
continuous-batching.example: "Continuous batching looked great until every long decode held KV memory hostage."
continuous-batching.misuse: Sold as infinite free throughput. Continuous batching still contends for memory, KV cache, and compute; overpacking raises latency for everyone.
continuous-batching.practicalMeaning: Watch batch size, queue time, TTFT, and tokens-per-second under mixed traffic. Tune max concurrent sequences and memory budgets rather than assuming the buzzword alone multiplies capacity.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Continuous batching is an inference-server scheduling technique that dynamically adds and removes requests from a running batch as prompts arrive and generations finish, keeping accelerators busier th

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Watch batch size, queue time, TTFT, and tokens-per-second under mixed traffic.

continuous-batching.warningLabel: Full GPUs are not the same as happy users.
continuous-batching.whyExists: Requests do not arrive in neat simultaneous groups, and generations end at different times. Static batches waste capacity waiting for stragglers or empty seats.

askNext: Are input and output priced differently, and what is your typical ratio?
Does the quote include cache hits, batch tiers, or only on-demand list price?
What is cost per completed user task after retries and tool calls?
cost-per-token.body: Cost per token is the menu price. The bill is still about appetite and waste.
cost-per-token.example: "Their cost-per-token looked unbeatable until the agent burned three retries correcting itself."
cost-per-token.misuse: Compared across vendors without matching context length, output ratio, caching, batch discounts, or quality. A cheaper token that forces more retries is not cheaper.
cost-per-token.practicalMeaning: Model total cost as input tokens, output tokens, cached tokens, tool calls, and failure retries. Track cost per successful task, not only list price per million tokens.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Cost per token is the price charged or estimated for processing or generating a token of text (or multimodal equivalent), usually quoted separately for input and output and used as a rough unit econom

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Model total cost as input tokens, output tokens, cached tokens, tool calls, and failure retries.

cost-per-token.warningLabel: Unit price without workload shape is theatre.
cost-per-token.whyExists: Usage-based model APIs needed a billable unit finer than seats or servers. Tokens became the common currency of inference spend.

askNext: Which metrics are governed in Cube, and who owns those definitions?
What data can the agent access through MCP, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Does the answer show the query, assumptions, and freshness of the data?
cube.body: Data agents sound glamorous until someone asks whether "active customer" means the same thing in finance, product, and the board deck.
cube.example: "The Cube MCP server helped the agent answer the revenue question with the company's definition of revenue, a small miracle in analytics politics."
cube.misuse: It gets treated as if connecting a model to analytics automatically creates trustworthy business intelligence. The quality still depends on the semantic model, access controls, freshness, metric definitions, and whether the question deserved a dashboard rather than a paragraph.
cube.practicalMeaning: In practice Cube means exposing governed metrics and analytics workflows to agents through a controlled interface, so data questions can use established definitions instead of freshly hallucinated SQL.

translations.label: data meaning
translations.text: Let agents ask questions through shared metric definitions instead of improvising business logic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cleaner bridge between AI clients and governed analytics, provided the semantic layer is actually maintained.

cube.warningLabel: A governed metric is only as good as the governance everyone keeps pretending happened.
cube.whyExists: It exists because business data is full of definitions, joins, permissions, and metrics that do not belong inside a model's imagination. A semantic layer gives agents a governed way to ask questions against shared business logic.

askNext: What exact meaning of CUDA is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
cuda.body: CUDA earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
cuda.example: "CUDA came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
cuda.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
cuda.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

cuda.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
cuda.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around NVIDIA often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: What exact meaning of Curation is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
curation.body: Curation gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Human Taste and Taste keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
curation.example: "Once curation entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
curation.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
curation.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

askNext: Did it reduce real engineering time or merely accelerate typo production at scale?
How much of the gain came from codebase retrieval versus raw generation?
Who is reviewing the edits with enough context to reject confident nonsense?
cursor.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.
cursor.example: "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."
cursor.misuse: By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
cursor.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

cursor.whyExists: Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
